frameworks.name: phrase-structure tree (merge operation)
frameworks.one_line: syntax built by recursive merge of words into nested phrases

frameworks.name: sequential transition probability
frameworks.one_line: language as linear lexical/syntactic-category transition statistics

core_question: Does brain activity during sentence reading reflect construction of nested phrase structures rather than mere sequential word transitions?
key_assumptions: syntax involves a tree of nested phrases, not a linear word sequence
high-gamma power indexes local linguistic computation
a single merge operation applies across phrases of arbitrary complexity
manipulations: sentences vs. word lists, presented word-by-word
successive word position within a sentence
boundaries where words can be merged into a phrase
model comparison: phrase construction vs. transition probability
key_findings: high-gamma power increased with each successive word in a sentence
high-gamma power decreased suddenly at phrase-merge boundaries
each added word or multiword phrase added similar amounts of activity
transition-probability models captured only posterior middle temporal gyrus activity
interpretations: left superior temporal and inferior frontal cortices implement a merge operation over linguistic objects of arbitrary complexity
phrase-structure construction fits neural activity better than surface sequential statistics at most language sites
